turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kagan has the opinion this morning in case 22-138, Counterman versus Colorado.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: For over two years, Billy Counterman sent hundreds of Facebook messages to CW, a singer and musician in Colorado. The two had never met and CW didn't respond. In fact, she repeatedly blocked Counterman, but each time, he created a new Facebook account and resumed his contacts. A number of his messages expressed anger at CW and said, she would suffer violent harm. Those communications terrified CW and abandoned her life. She stopped walking alone, declined social engagements, and canceled some of her performances. Colorado brought criminal charges against Counterman for those messages. Under state law, the prosecutor had to prove that Counterman's messages were objectively threatening, that a reasonable person would understand the messages as threatening violent hard to CW. But the prosecutor didn't have to prove that Counterman had an intent to threaten, or even had an awareness that the messages would be understood as threatening. On that basis, a jury convicted Counterman of sending threatening messages. On appeal, Counterman argued that his conviction violated the First Amendment. He maintained that the Constitution required Colorado to show not only that his statements were objectively threatening, but also that he intended or at least was aware of their threatening character. The Colorado Court of Appeal rejected that argument, but other courts have come out differently on the issue. So we decided to hear the case. Today, we hold that in so-called true threats cases, the First Amendment requires proof that the defendant had some subjective understanding of the threatening nature of his statements, but that does not mean the state has to show the defendant specifically intended to threaten another person. It is enough if the state shows the defendant acted recklessly, that he consciously disregarded a substantial risk that his communications would be viewed, as threatening violence. We begin by reaffirming the true threats of violence are not themselves protected by the First Amendment. Some categories of speech, incitement, defamation, obscenity have not been historically protected, because they have so little value and cause so much harm. Another one of those historically unprotected categories is true threats. Serious expressions conveying that the speaker means to commit an act of unlawful violence against another. And what is a true threat does not depend on the speaker's mindset. It depends solely on what his words convey. And yet the First Amendment still demands a subjective mental state element shielding some true threats from punishment. That's because in the absence of such a requirement, valuable speech, non-threatening speech, might be chilled or deterred. A speaker might be uncertain about whether his statements are threats, or he may worry that the legal system will say they're threats even though they are not, or he may fear the expense of becoming entangled in the legal process. So he steers wide of the danger zone. He engages in a kind of self-censorship, and he does that even though his statements are not true threats. To prevent that from happening, we insist that the state in a true threats case has to show some kind of culpable mental state. The idea is that imposing that requirement will reduce the chilling effect on protected non-threatening speech. In demanding that much, we treat true threats as we have other unprotected categories of speech, defamation, obscenity, incitement. In all those areas, our precedent demands that the state make a showing about what the defendant had in his mind, and we do that again here to ensure that the prosecution is unprotected speech doesn't work to chill valuable protected expression. The next question is what kind of subjective showing the state has to make, and on that issue, we have to strike a balance, because even as we want to reduce the chill of protected speech, we don't want to make it too hard for prosecutors to punish people for true threats of violence, which are terribly damaging in all the ways the facts of this case show. Looking again to our precedent, especially about defamation, we decide that the constitutionally required mental state standard is recklessness. That standard offers grieving space for protected speech without sacrificing too much in the way of enforcing laws against unprotected true threats. Here Colorado prosecuted Counterman without showing that he was aware of his statements' threatening character. That is a violation of the First Amendment. If the state decides to re-try Counterman, it must show that he consciously disregarded a substantial risk that his communications would be viewed as threatening violence. Justice Sotomayor has filed an opinion concurring in part and concurring in the judgment, in which Justice Gorsuch joins in part. Justice Thomas has filed a dissenting opinion. Justice Barrett has filed a dissenting opinion in which Justice Thomas joins.
